name: Gaeng Keow Wan Tofu (Green Curry Tofu)
desc: Creamy Thai green curry with tofu, bamboo shoots, and Thai basil in coconut milk.
steps: Press tofu and cut into cubes. Simmer green curry paste in a splash of coconut milk for 2 minutes.
Add remaining coconut milk, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Simmer 15 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, stir in Thai basil, and serve over jasmine rice.
tips: Use extra-firm tofu for better texture.
Add Thai eggplant for authenticity.

name: Thai Peanut Noodles
desc: Rice noodles tossed in a savory-sweet peanut sauce with crunchy vegetables and lime.
steps: Cook rice noodles per package directions, drain and rinse with cold water.
Whisk peanut butter, soy sauce, lime juice, and a splash of warm water into a smooth sauce.
Toss noodles with sauce, julienned carrot, sliced cucumber, and top with crushed peanuts.
tips: Add sriracha for heat.
Chill before serving for a refreshing cold noodle dish.

name: Thai Coconut Chicken Soup (Tom Kha Variation)
desc: Silky coconut broth with chicken, mushrooms, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves.
steps: Simmer lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves in coconut milk for 10 minutes.
Add sliced chicken and mushrooms, cook until chicken is done, about 12 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and lime juice, garnish with cilantro.
tips: Bruise lemongrass stalks to release more flavor.
Remove aromatics before serving if preferred.

name: Thai Basil Fried Rice (Khao Pad Kra Pao)
desc: Smoky wok-fried rice with Thai holy basil, chili, garlic, and a fried egg on top.
steps: Heat oil in wok over high heat. Fry garlic and chilies until fragrant.
Add rice, toss vigorously with soy sauce until heated through and slightly charred.
Stir in Thai basil until wilted. Serve topped with a fried egg.
tips: Use day-old rice for best results.
Holy basil is preferred but sweet basil works too.

name: Thai Red Curry with Shrimp
desc: Plump shrimp simmered in a rich red curry coconut sauce with bell peppers and Thai basil.
steps: Fry red curry paste in a splash of coconut milk until fragrant, about 2 minutes.
Add remaining coconut milk, sliced bell pepper, and shrimp. Simmer 8 minutes until shrimp are pink.
Season with fish sauce, stir in Thai basil, and serve with rice.
tips: Devein shrimp for cleaner presentation.
Add bamboo shoots or pea eggplants for variety.

name: Nam Tok Moo (Thai Grilled Pork Salad)
desc: Chargrilled pork slices tossed with toasted rice powder, herbs, lime, and chili flakes.
steps: Grill pork over high heat until charred and cooked through, about 12 minutes. Rest and slice thinly.
Toss sliced pork with lime juice, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder.
Add cilantro and mint, toss gently, serve immediately with sticky rice.
tips: Make toasted rice powder by dry-roasting jasmine rice and grinding.
Pork neck is the traditional cut for this dish.

name: Pad Woon Sen (Stir-Fried Glass Noodles)
desc: Bouncy glass noodles stir-fried with egg, vegetables, and a savory soy-oyster sauce.
steps: Soak glass noodles in warm water 10 minutes, drain. Scramble eggs in oiled wok, set aside.
Stir-fry garlic, shredded carrot, and cabbage for 2 minutes on high heat.
Add noodles, soy sauce, oyster sauce, scrambled egg, toss until well combined and heated through.
tips: Don't oversoak noodles or they'll become mushy.
Add shrimp or chicken for protein.

name: Thai Crab Fried Rice (Khao Pad Poo)
desc: Wok-fried jasmine rice with lump crab meat, egg, and a squeeze of lime.
steps: Heat oil in wok, scramble eggs, push to side. Fry garlic until golden.
Add rice and crab meat, toss on high heat with soy sauce for 3 minutes.
Squeeze lime over top, garnish with sliced green onions, serve with cucumber slices.
tips: Use day-old rice for best wok hei.
Fresh crab is ideal but canned works in a pinch.

name: Yam Woon Sen (Thai Glass Noodle Salad)
desc: Tangy glass noodle salad with shrimp, pork, lime dressing, and fresh herbs.
steps: Soak glass noodles, drain. Poach shrimp and pork separately until cooked. Let cool.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, and minced chili.
Toss noodles, shrimp, pork with dressing, add cilantro and sliced shallots, serve at room temperature.
tips: Serve immediately as noodles absorb dressing quickly.
Add celery for crunch.

name: Gai Yang (Thai Grilled Chicken)
desc: Juicy Thai-style grilled chicken marinated in lemongrass, garlic, cilantro root, and pepper.
steps: Blend lemongrass, garlic, cilantro root, pepper, soy sauce, and palm sugar into a paste. Marinate chicken for 30 minutes.
Grill over medium heat, turning occasionally, until charred and cooked through, about 25 minutes.
Rest 5 minutes, chop, and serve with sticky rice and sweet chili sauce.
tips: Marinate overnight for deeper flavor.
Flatten chicken pieces for even cooking.

name: Pad Pak Boong (Stir-Fried Morning Glory)
desc: Fiery wok-tossed water spinach with garlic, chili, and fermented soybean paste.
steps: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Add crushed garlic and sliced chili, fry 10 seconds.
Add morning glory, toss rapidly on high heat for 1 minute.
Season with soy sauce and oyster sauce, toss once more, and serve immediately.
tips: The wok must be extremely hot for the signature smoky flavor.
Substitute with regular spinach if morning glory is unavailable.

name: Khai Jiao (Thai Omelette)
desc: Puffy, crispy-edged Thai omelette fried in plenty of oil, served over rice with chili sauce.
steps: Beat eggs vigorously with fish sauce, soy sauce, and white pepper until frothy.
Heat generous oil in wok until very hot. Pour in egg mixture, let puff and crisp for 1 minute.
Fold omelette, drain briefly, serve over jasmine rice with sriracha.
tips: High heat and plenty of oil is the secret to the signature puffiness.
Add minced pork for Khai Jiao Moo Sap.

name: Kuay Teow Reua (Thai Boat Noodle Soup)
desc: Intensely flavored dark broth with rice noodles, beef, herbs, and a splash of vinegar.
steps: Simmer beef with star anise, cinnamon, and dark soy sauce in water for 30 minutes. Slice beef thinly.
Cook rice noodles, divide into bowls. Ladle hot broth over noodles and sliced beef.
Top with bean sprouts, cilantro, Thai basil, and a splash of white vinegar.
tips: Traditionally served in small bowls for concentrated flavor.
Add pork liver or meatballs for authenticity.

name: Tod Man Khao Pod (Thai Corn Fritters)
desc: Crispy golden corn fritters seasoned with red curry paste and kaffir lime leaves.
steps: Mix corn, curry paste, finely sliced kaffir lime leaves, rice flour, and egg into a thick batter.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil, fry until golden and crispy, about 3 minutes per side.
Drain on paper towels and serve with sweet chili sauce.
tips: Fresh corn gives the best sweetness and crunch.
Flatten fritters slightly for even cooking.

name: Pad Kee Mao (Drunken Noodles)
desc: Spicy, smoky wide rice noodles stir-fried with Thai basil, chili, and garlic.
steps: Soak noodles in warm water until pliable. Stir-fry sliced chicken in hot wok until cooked, set aside.
In same wok, fry garlic and chili, add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Toss on high heat.
Return chicken, add Thai basil, toss until wilted and serve immediately.
tips: The key is extremely high heat for wok hei.
Use flat sen yai noodles for authenticity.

name: Gaeng Liang (Thai Pumpkin Curry Soup)
desc: Fragrant shrimp paste-based curry soup with pumpkin, corn, and Thai basil.
steps: Pound shallots, white pepper, and shrimp paste into a rough paste.
Boil water, add paste, cubed pumpkin, and corn. Simmer until pumpkin is tender, about 15 minutes.
Add shrimp, cook 3 minutes, stir in Thai basil and serve.
tips: This is one of Thailand's healthiest traditional soups.
Use butternut squash as a pumpkin substitute.

name: Pla Rad Prik (Thai Fried Fish with Chili Sauce)
desc: Crispy deep-fried whole fish topped with a tangy, sweet, and spicy tamarind-chili sauce.
steps: Score fish on both sides, pat dry, dust with rice flour. Deep fry until golden and crispy.
In a separate pan, fry garlic and chili, add tamarind, palm sugar, fish sauce, simmer until thick.
Pour sauce over crispy fish, garnish with crispy Thai basil leaves.
tips: Dry the fish thoroughly for maximum crispiness.
Sea bass or tilapia work well for this dish.

name: Gai Pad Med Mamuang (Thai Cashew Chicken)
desc: Tender chicken stir-fried with roasted cashews, dried chilies, and a sweet soy glaze.
steps: Stir-fry diced chicken in hot oil until golden. Add garlic and dried chilies, fry 30 seconds.
Add onion wedges, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Toss for 2 minutes.
Stir in cashews, toss to combine, and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Toast cashews separately first for extra crunch.
Don't burn the dried chilies.

name: Som Tum Poo (Green Papaya Salad with Crab)
desc: Punchy shredded papaya salad with salted crab, tomato, long beans, and peanuts.
steps: Shred green papaya. In a mortar, pound garlic, chili, and long beans lightly.
Add salted crab, tomato, palm sugar, lime juice, and fish sauce. Pound gently to mix.
Add shredded papaya, toss and pound lightly. Top with crushed peanuts.
tips: This is the Isaan-style version with crab.
Adjust chili to taste — it should be spicy, sour, and sweet.

name: Pla Neung Manao (Steamed Fish with Lime)
desc: Delicate steamed fish fillet topped with a fiery lime, garlic, and chili dressing.
steps: Place fish on a plate, steam over boiling water for 12 minutes until flaky.
Pound garlic and chili in mortar, mix with lime juice, fish sauce, and palm sugar.
Pour dressing over steamed fish, garnish with cilantro and serve.
tips: Use sea bass for the most authentic version.
The dressing should be intensely sour, salty, and spicy.

name: Moo Ping (Thai Grilled Pork Skewers)
desc: Sweet and savory grilled pork skewers marinated in coconut milk, garlic, and palm sugar.
steps: Slice pork thinly, marinate in coconut milk, garlic, palm sugar, fish sauce, and pepper for 30 minutes.
Thread onto bamboo skewers, grill over medium-high heat for 3-4 minutes per side.
Serve with sticky rice and jaew dipping sauce.
tips: Soak bamboo skewers in water first to prevent burning.
Pork shoulder gives the best fat-to-meat ratio.

name: Kanom Jeen Nam Ya (Thai Rice Noodles with Fish Curry)
desc: Fermented rice noodles served with a rich, herbal fish curry sauce and fresh vegetables.
steps: Boil and flake fish. Blend fish, red curry paste, krachai, and a splash of coconut milk into a paste.
Simmer paste with remaining coconut milk for 15 minutes until thickened and fragrant.
Serve curry over rice noodles with raw bean sprouts, long beans, and Thai basil on the side.
tips: Krachai (fingerroot) gives the authentic flavor but galangal can substitute.
This is a beloved Thai street food lunch.

name: Larb Moo (Thai Minced Pork Salad)
desc: Zesty minced pork salad with toasted rice powder, fresh mint, cilantro, and fierce chili.
steps: Cook ground pork in a dry pan until just done, breaking into small pieces. Let cool slightly.
Toss with lime juice, fish sauce, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder.
Fold in mint and cilantro, serve at room temperature with sticky rice and cabbage leaves.
tips: Don't overcook the pork — it should be juicy.
Toasted rice powder adds the essential nutty crunch.

name: Gaeng Som (Thai Sour Curry)
desc: Tangy, spicy southern Thai sour curry with shrimp, vegetables, and tamarind broth.
steps: Bring water to boil, dissolve sour curry paste. Add tamarind, palm sugar, and fish sauce.
Add chopped green beans and cabbage, simmer 8 minutes until tender.
Add shrimp, cook 3 minutes, taste and adjust sour-sweet balance.
tips: This curry has NO coconut milk — it's meant to be light and tangy.
Thai sour curry paste is different from red curry paste.

name: Moo Grob (Thai Crispy Pork Belly)
desc: Shatteringly crispy pork belly with crackling skin, often served over rice with chili vinegar.
steps: Score pork skin, rub flesh side with five-spice and pepper. Rub skin with vinegar, salt, and baking soda.
Roast at 200°C skin-side up for 60 minutes, then blast at 240°C for 15 minutes until skin puffs and crackles.
Rest 10 minutes, slice into pieces, serve over rice with sweet chili or chili vinegar.
tips: Pat the skin completely dry before seasoning for maximum crackle.
Poke holes in the skin with a fork for even puffing.

name: Peek Gai Tod (Thai Fried Chicken Wings)
desc: Double-fried crispy chicken wings tossed in a sticky sweet chili fish sauce glaze.
steps: Marinate wings in fish sauce and garlic for 15 minutes. Coat in rice flour.
Deep fry at 160°C for 8 minutes, rest 5 minutes, then fry again at 190°C for 3 minutes until super crispy.
Toss in sweet chili sauce and serve immediately.
tips: Double frying is the key to lasting crispiness.
Rice flour gives a lighter, crunchier crust than wheat flour.

name: Khao Man Gai (Thai Chicken Rice)
desc: Silky poached chicken served over fragrant garlic-ginger rice with a tangy soybean dipping sauce.
steps: Poach chicken in gently simmering salted water with ginger slices for 25 minutes. Reserve broth.
Fry garlic in chicken fat, add rice, toast 2 minutes. Cook rice with chicken broth.
Slice chicken, serve over rice with cucumber slices and soybean-chili-ginger dipping sauce.
tips: Ice bath the chicken after poaching for silky, jiggly skin.
This is Thailand's version of Hainanese chicken rice.

name: Phat Phak Bung Fai Daeng (Fire-Roasted Morning Glory)
desc: Wok-tossed morning glory with garlic and chili in a dramatic flame technique.
steps: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Flash-fry garlic, chili, and soybean paste for 10 seconds.
Throw in morning glory, toss vigorously for 30 seconds on highest heat.
Season with soy sauce, toss once more, serve immediately.
tips: This dish must be cooked extremely fast on very high heat.
Known as 'flying vegetable' when tossed from wok to plate.

name: Massaman Beef Stew
desc: Slow-braised beef in a rich massaman curry with potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices.
steps: Sear beef chunks, set aside. Fry curry paste in coconut cream until oil separates.
Add beef, coconut milk, potatoes, tamarind, and palm sugar. Simmer gently for 90 minutes.
Add peanuts in the last 10 minutes. Serve with jasmine rice.
tips: Low and slow is the key — don't rush the simmer.
Massaman curry paste includes cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves.

name: Khanom Tuay (Thai Coconut Pudding)
desc: Silky two-layered coconut pudding with a sweet bottom and salty coconut cream top.
steps: Mix rice flour, sugar, and half the coconut milk with pandan water to make the sweet layer. Steam in small cups for 10 minutes.
Mix remaining coconut milk with a pinch of salt and a little rice flour for the savory top layer.
Pour salty layer on top of set sweet layer, steam 5 more minutes until set.
tips: The contrast of sweet bottom and salty top is the signature.
Use fresh pandan for the best color and aroma.

name: Gluay Buat Chi (Bananas in Coconut Milk)
desc: Warm poached bananas in sweet, salted coconut milk — a classic Thai comfort dessert.
steps: Bring coconut milk, sugar, salt, and pandan leaves to a gentle simmer.
Add halved bananas, poach gently for 5 minutes until just tender.
Serve warm in bowls with the coconut milk broth.
tips: Use slightly underripe bananas so they hold their shape.
Nam Wa bananas are traditional but any short variety works.

name: Gai Hor Bai Toey (Pandan Chicken)
desc: Marinated chicken wrapped in pandan leaves and deep-fried until fragrant and crispy.
steps: Marinate diced chicken in soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and garlic for 20 minutes.
Wrap each piece in a pandan leaf, securing with a toothpick.
Deep fry parcels for 5-6 minutes until golden. Serve with sweet chili sauce.
tips: The pandan leaf infuses an incredible aroma into the chicken.
Don't unwrap before eating — pull the chicken out of the leaf.

name: Gaeng Hung Lay (Northern Thai Pork Curry)
desc: Rich Burmese-influenced pork curry with ginger, tamarind, and warm spices from Chiang Mai.
steps: Simmer cubed pork belly in hung lay paste with ginger, tamarind, and palm sugar for 45 minutes.
Add peanuts and pickled garlic in the last 10 minutes.
Serve with steamed jasmine rice — the sauce should be thick and glossy.
tips: This is Chiang Mai's signature curry, distinct from central Thai curries.
The flavor should be sweet-sour-savory without coconut milk.

name: Pla Meuk Yang (Thai Grilled Squid)
desc: Chargrilled whole squid basted with a sweet soy glaze, served with seafood dipping sauce.
steps: Clean squid, score the body in a crosshatch pattern. Mix soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and garlic into a glaze.
Grill squid over high heat, basting with glaze, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Slice and serve with nam jim seafood dipping sauce and lime wedges.
tips: Don't overcook — squid goes from tender to rubbery in seconds.
A common Thai street food, best from charcoal grills.

name: Khao Kluk Kapi (Shrimp Paste Fried Rice)
desc: Aromatic fried rice with shrimp paste served with sweet pork, dried shrimp, and mango.
steps: Fry shrimp paste in oil until fragrant. Add rice and toss on high heat for 3 minutes.
Push rice aside, scramble egg in the wok, then mix together.
Serve rice mound surrounded by sliced green mango, fried shallots, dried shrimp, and sweet pork.
tips: This is a composed dish — each element is placed around the rice.
The shrimp paste gives an intense umami base.

name: Nam Tok Neua (Thai Beef Waterfall Salad)
desc: Grilled beef salad with charred edges, tossed in lime, fish sauce, and toasted rice powder.
steps: Grill steak over high heat to medium-rare, about 4 minutes per side. Rest and slice thinly against the grain.
Toss sliced beef with lime juice, fish sauce, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder.
Fold in fresh mint, serve immediately with sticky rice.
tips: The name 'waterfall' refers to the juices dripping during grilling.
Medium-rare is essential for tender slices.

name: Pad King Tofu (Stir-Fried Tofu with Ginger)
desc: Crispy tofu stir-fried with julienned ginger, mushrooms, and a light soy-oyster sauce.
steps: Pan-fry cubed tofu until golden on all sides. Set aside.
Stir-fry julienned ginger and sliced mushrooms for 2 minutes. Add bell pepper.
Return tofu, add soy sauce and oyster sauce, toss. Garnish with green onions.
tips: Fresh young ginger is milder and works best here.
Press tofu well for crispier results.

name: Gaeng Pa (Thai Jungle Curry)
desc: Fiery coconut-free curry packed with vegetables, herbs, and green peppercorns.
steps: Fry jungle curry paste in oil until fragrant. Add sliced chicken, stir-fry 3 minutes.
Add water, bamboo shoots, long beans, and green peppercorns. Simmer 12 minutes.
Stir in Thai basil, season with fish sauce. Serve with rice.
tips: This is a NO-coconut-milk curry — thin, spicy broth.
Green peppercorns give the distinctive tongue-numbing heat.

name: Goong Makham (Thai Tamarind Shrimp)
desc: Large shrimp glazed in a caramelized sweet-sour tamarind sauce with crispy shallots.
steps: Fry shallots until crispy, set aside. In same pan, sear shrimp until pink, set aside.
Make sauce: simmer tamarind, palm sugar, fish sauce, and garlic until thick and glossy.
Toss shrimp in sauce, plate and top with crispy shallots.
tips: The sauce should coat the back of a spoon — thick and sticky.
Use head-on shrimp for the most dramatic presentation.

name: Pad Kra Pao Goong (Thai Basil Shrimp)
desc: Wok-fired shrimp with holy basil, chili, and garlic in a savory sauce served over rice.
steps: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Stir-fry garlic and chili for 10 seconds.
Add shrimp, toss on high heat until pink, about 2 minutes.
Add soy sauce, oyster sauce, and holy basil. Toss until basil wilts, serve over rice.
tips: Holy basil gives the authentic peppery flavor.
Cook fast on high heat — shrimp overcook quickly.

name: Ice Cream Kati (Thai Coconut Ice Cream)
desc: Rich coconut milk ice cream served in a coconut shell with peanuts and sweet corn.
steps: Heat coconut milk with sugar and salt until dissolved. Chill completely.
Churn in an ice cream maker until thick and creamy, about 20 minutes.
Serve scoops topped with sweet corn, crushed peanuts, and a spoonful of sticky rice.
tips: No ice cream maker? Freeze in a pan, stirring every 30 minutes.
The peanut-corn-sticky rice combo is the traditional topping.

name: Gaeng Phed Ped Yang (Red Curry Roast Duck)
desc: Succulent roast duck in red curry with pineapple, tomatoes, and Thai basil.
steps: Fry red curry paste in coconut cream until oil separates. Add remaining coconut milk.
Add sliced roast duck, pineapple chunks, and halved tomatoes. Simmer 10 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, stir in Thai basil, serve with steamed rice.
tips: Buy ready-roasted duck from a Chinese BBQ shop.
The pineapple adds essential sweetness to balance the curry.

name: Yam Som O (Thai Pomelo Salad)
desc: Refreshing pomelo segments tossed with toasted coconut, dried shrimp, peanuts, and chili.
steps: Peel pomelo and break into segments, then shred into small pieces.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, palm sugar, and chili.
Toss pomelo with dressing, toasted coconut, dried shrimp, peanuts, and sliced shallots.
tips: Use pink pomelo for the prettiest presentation.
This is a common festive Thai dish.

name: Yam Pla Dook Foo (Crispy Catfish Salad)
desc: Fluffy shredded deep-fried catfish served with a spicy green mango salad.
steps: Grill or steam catfish, flake meat finely. Deep fry flaked fish until golden and puffy.
Shred green mango, slice shallots and chili. Mix with lime juice and fish sauce.
Mound crispy fish on plate, top with green mango salad, serve immediately.
tips: The catfish should be extremely fluffy after frying.
Eat quickly — the crispiness fades fast.

name: Khao Niao Durian (Sticky Rice with Durian)
desc: Warm sticky rice with rich coconut cream and fresh durian — a polarizing Thai delicacy.
steps: Soak sticky rice 4 hours, then steam until tender, about 20 minutes.
Heat coconut milk with sugar and salt. Mix half into the hot sticky rice.
Serve sticky rice with durian flesh and drizzle remaining coconut sauce on top.
tips: Use fresh durian for the most authentic experience.
This is as beloved in Thailand as mango sticky rice.

name: Moo Tod Kratiem (Thai Garlic Pepper Pork)
desc: Golden fried pork marinated with garlic, black pepper, and cilantro root, served over rice.
steps: Pound garlic, pepper, and cilantro root into a paste. Marinate sliced pork with paste and soy sauce for 15 minutes.
Pan-fry pork slices in hot oil until golden and cooked through, about 4 minutes per side.
Serve over rice with cucumber slices and a fried egg if desired.
tips: Cilantro root is essential for the authentic Thai flavor.
Don't skip the black pepper — it's a key element.

name: Massaman Gai (Massaman Chicken Curry)
desc: Tender chicken simmered in a mild, aromatic massaman curry with potato and onion.
steps: Fry massaman paste in coconut cream until fragrant. Add chicken, sear 3 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk, add chunked potatoes and quartered onion. Simmer 30 minutes.
Add peanuts and palm sugar, cook 5 more minutes. Serve with rice.
tips: Massaman is the mildest Thai curry — perfect for spice-sensitive eaters.
Originally Muslim-influenced, it uses warm spices like cinnamon and star anise.

name: Tom Yum Goong Nam Khon (Creamy Tom Yum Shrimp)
desc: The creamy version of tom yum with shrimp, mushrooms, and a rich chili-coconut broth.
steps: Simmer lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves in water for 10 minutes.
Add mushrooms, shrimp, and nam prik pao (roasted chili paste). Cook until shrimp are pink.
Stir in coconut milk, squeeze in lime juice. Serve garnished with cilantro.
tips: Nam prik pao is the key to the creamy version — it adds richness and color.
Don't boil after adding coconut milk.

name: Gaeng Keow Wan Gai (Green Curry Chicken)
desc: Classic Thai green curry with tender chicken, bamboo shoots, and Thai eggplant in coconut milk.
steps: Fry green curry paste in coconut cream until fragrant. Add chicken, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add eggplant and bamboo shoots. Simmer 15 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar, stir in Thai basil, serve with rice.
tips: Thai eggplant are small and round — use regular eggplant if unavailable.
The curry should be rich and creamy, not watery.

name: Yam Mamuang (Thai Green Mango Salad)
desc: Crunchy shredded green mango tossed with dried shrimp, peanuts, and a tangy lime dressing.
steps: Shred green mango into thin strips. Slice shallots thinly.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, and palm sugar.
Toss mango, shallots, dried shrimp, and peanuts with dressing. Serve immediately.
tips: The mango must be unripe — sour and crunchy.
Add fresh chili for heat.

name: Gaeng Garee Gai (Yellow Curry Chicken)
desc: Mild, turmeric-golden chicken curry with potatoes and onion in a creamy coconut base.
steps: Fry yellow curry paste and turmeric in coconut cream until fragrant. Add chicken, sear 3 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk, add cubed potatoes and sliced onion. Simmer 25 minutes.
Season with fish sauce. Serve with rice or roti canai.
tips: This is the mildest Thai curry — great for beginners.
Traditionally served with roti bread for dipping.

name: Tom Zab (Thai Northeastern Pork Rib Soup)
desc: Clear, intensely herbal Isaan pork rib soup with lemongrass, galangal, and dill.
steps: Simmer pork ribs in water with lemongrass and galangal for 30 minutes until tender.
Season with fish sauce and lime juice. Add crushed chili.
Stir in fresh dill just before serving. Serve with sticky rice.
tips: Dill is the signature herb of Isaan soups — don't substitute.
The broth should be clear, sour, and intensely herbal.

name: Khao Soi Gai (Chiang Mai Curry Noodles)
desc: Northern Thai coconut curry soup with egg noodles, chicken, and crispy noodle topping.
steps: Fry curry paste with turmeric in coconut cream. Add chicken, coat well, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk and water, simmer 25 minutes until chicken is tender.
Serve over egg noodles, topped with crispy fried noodles, sliced shallots, and pickled mustard greens.
tips: Reserve some noodles, fry until crispy for the essential topping.
This is Chiang Mai's most famous noodle dish.

name: Sangkaya Fak Thong (Pumpkin Custard)
desc: Silky coconut custard steamed inside a whole pumpkin — a beloved Thai dessert.
steps: Cut top off pumpkin, scoop out seeds. Whisk eggs with coconut milk, palm sugar, salt, and pandan extract.
Pour custard into pumpkin, replace lid, steam for 45 minutes until custard is set.
Cool, slice pumpkin into wedges so each piece has custard attached.
tips: The pumpkin must be small enough to fit in your steamer.
The custard should be just set — not over-steamed.

name: Khao Niao Sang Kaya (Sticky Rice with Egg Custard)
desc: Sweet coconut sticky rice paired with a rich, firm pandan-scented egg custard.
steps: Soak sticky rice, steam until tender. Mix with half the coconut milk, sugar, and salt.
Whisk eggs with remaining coconut milk, palm sugar, and pandan extract. Steam custard for 20 minutes.
Serve warm sticky rice with sliced custard on the side.
tips: The custard should be firm enough to slice.
A Thai market staple, often sold in banana leaf wraps.

name: Kor Moo Yang (Thai Grilled Pork Neck)
desc: Juicy chargrilled pork neck marinated in garlic, lemongrass, and fish sauce.
steps: Blend garlic, lemongrass, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and palm sugar. Marinate pork neck for 20 minutes.
Grill over medium heat, turning every 5 minutes, until charred and cooked through, about 20 minutes.
Rest 5 minutes, slice across the grain, serve with sticky rice and jaew dipping sauce.
tips: Pork neck has the perfect fat content for grilling.
This is Thailand's most popular street food grilled meat.

name: Pad Kra Pao Gai Sap (Thai Basil Minced Chicken)
desc: Lightning-fast stir-fry of minced chicken with holy basil, chili, and garlic over rice.
steps: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Fry garlic and crushed chili 10 seconds.
Add ground chicken, break apart, stir-fry on high heat until cooked, about 3 minutes.
Add soy sauce, oyster sauce, and holy basil. Toss until basil wilts, serve over rice with a fried egg.
tips: This is Thailand's most-eaten weekday lunch.
Holy basil is non-negotiable for the authentic version.

name: Yam Tofu Tod (Crispy Tofu Salad)
desc: Golden fried tofu cubes tossed in a spicy lime dressing with fresh herbs and roasted chili.
steps: Cut tofu into cubes, deep fry until golden and crispy on all sides.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, and roasted chili flakes.
Toss crispy tofu with dressing, sliced shallots, and cilantro. Serve immediately.
tips: Serve quickly before tofu loses crispiness.
Add minced pork for a heartier version.

name: Khai Pad Kra Pao (Stir-Fried Egg with Basil)
desc: Simple scrambled eggs stir-fried with holy basil, chili, and oyster sauce over rice.
steps: Heat oil in wok. Fry garlic and crushed chili for 10 seconds.
Crack in eggs, let set slightly, then scramble on high heat for 1 minute.
Add oyster sauce and Thai basil, toss until basil wilts, serve over rice.
tips: Don't over-scramble — eggs should be slightly runny.
A quick, cheap Thai street food meal.
